6: A direct revelation: 'God who said Let light shine out of darkness has shone in our hearts to give the light of the knowledge of the glory of God in the face of Jesus Christ.' The glory of God — YHWH's Shekinah-presence, the consuming fire, the cloud-pillar, the heavenly radiance — is now specifically located: in the face of Jesus Christ. This is Paul's most explicit identification of Jesus with the divine glory. Conversion is a new-creation event (2 Corinthians 4:6 echoes Genesis 1:3) in which God's glory, once accessible only in the tabernacle and temple, is now encountered in the person of Christ.
1: The apostle's perseverance is grounded not in personal resilience but in the mercy (eleos) that constitutes the ministry. Christ the merciful high priest (Hebrews 2:17) is both the source and the model: he endured the cross for the joy set before him (Hebrews 12:2), so the apostle does not lose heart.
2: Apostolic transparency mirrors the incarnation's plainness: Christ did not commend himself through clever rhetoric but through self-giving love (1 Corinthians 2:1-5). Renouncing underhanded ways is the apostle's participation in the character of the one who concealed nothing.
3: The veil remains over those who reject the crucified Christ. The stumbling block of the cross (1 Corinthians 1:23) is the veil; those who will not bow before the crucified King cannot see the glory that shines in his face.
4: Christ is the eikōn tou theou (image of God — Colossians 1:15), the one in whom the invisible God becomes fully visible (John 14:9). Satan's blinding strategy aims at preventing the unveiling of this image; the incarnation and resurrection are God's definitive unveiling of himself in the face of his Son.
5: The proclamation of Christ as kyrios (Lord) is the Easter confession (Philippians 2:11: Jesus Christ is Lord, to the glory of God the Father). The apostle's entire task is derivative: he witnesses to the risen Lord, not to himself. Self-proclamation would be a fundamental misunderstanding of the mission.
7: The treasure is Christ himself dwelling in the apostle (Colossians 1:27: Christ in you, the hope of glory). The clay jar conceals and reveals simultaneously: the weakness of the vessel contrasts with the power of its content, showing unmistakably that the resurrection power is divine, not human.
8: Each antithesis in 2 Corinthians 4:8-9 is a death-and-resurrection moment: pressed but not crushed, perplexed but not despairing. The limit that is never crossed is the limit the risen Christ holds — the same Christ who bore death itself without being consumed by it.
9: The apostle is never forsaken because Christ bore that forsakenness at Golgotha (Matthew 27:46: My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?). The striking down that does not destroy is the apostle's participation in the Easter pattern: crucified but raised, down but not out.
10: The two movements of the paschal mystery are enacted simultaneously in the apostle's body: the dying of Jesus (nekrosis — real physical suffering) and the life of Jesus (resurrection power manifested through that dying). The cross and the empty tomb are both on display in apostolic ministry.
11: Death first, then life — the paschal sequence is the apostolic pattern. The mortal flesh (thnete sarx) is the precise location where resurrection power operates, guaranteeing that the glory belongs entirely to God and not to the vessel.
12: The apostle's death-in-body generates life in the congregation — the cruciform dynamic of Christian ministry. As Christ's death generates life for the world, apostolic suffering generates life for the church. This is not coincidental but structurally necessary to the cross-shaped mission.
13: Psalms 116:10 (LXX: I believed, therefore I spoke) is read as the apostle's own confession and as an echo of Christ's own trust through the cross. The psalmist gave thanks for deliverance from death; Paul identifies with that trust-in-death and with the resurrection that followed.
14: The resurrection of Jesus is the guarantee of the apostle's resurrection — the same power that raised Christ from the dead will raise those who are in him (Romans 8:11). The final gathering before God is the eschatological completion of the body-of-Christ unity that already exists.
15: The extension of grace is the fruit of the cruciform pattern. As Christ's death was for all (2 Corinthians 5:14-15), so the apostle's suffering is for the congregation; the multiplication of grace through suffering produces an ever-expanding chorus of thanksgiving, gloria in excelsis.
16: The outer wasting is the dying of Jesus carried in the body; the inner renewal is the Spirit's resurrection work (Romans 8:10-11: if Christ is in you, though the body is dead, the Spirit gives life). The daily renewal is the Spirit progressively enacting the resurrection life that will be fully revealed at the last day.
17: The affliction is light not by denial but by comparison with the eschatological glory — the Christ-conforming transformation of the resurrection body (Philippians 3:21: he will transform our lowly body to be like his glorious body; 1 Corinthians 15:43: raised in glory). Present suffering is the path toward that sharing in Christ's own resurrection glory.
18: The unseen things are the risen and ascended Christ, the coming kingdom, and the resurrection life. Faith is the conviction of things not seen (Hebrews 11:1); the apostle's endurance is sustained by the vision of what Christ's resurrection has already secured and what his parousia will fully reveal.
